metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

auto-executed-tool-call asserts the auto-approved happy path: the agent uses one or more tools mid-turn and completes WITHOUT ever blocking on user input — the canonical arc is `ready → working → ready` with a tool_use inside the working span and the invariant that state never enters `waiting`. Distinct from the sibling full-lifecycle-toolcall (same coverage_id), which deliberately surfaces waiting via a permission gate.

OpenCode supports tool calls (capabilities.json tool_calls:true). In headless default (`opencode run`), tools auto-execute inside OpenCode's own sandbox with no interactive permission prompt, so a single tool-using turn produces exactly the ungated ready→working→ready arc. Session state lives in OpenCode's SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db); the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the UUID-keyed ses_… session.

The tool call leaves an observable trace: OpenCode writes a `tool` part to the part table that progresses pending → running → completed/error, and the opencode parser maps it to function_call (pending/running) and function_call_output (completed/error) events (parser.go parseToolPart). The turn settles working→ready when the parser sees a step-finish part with reason="stop" → turn_done (parser.go parseStepFinish). The phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the auto-executed tool call is asserted via the single sustained working span plus the `no state_transition to waiting` invariant, and verified out-of-band in the SQLite store / transcript.

MODEL note: opencode's default model is the local LM Studio model lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored (set in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json). The driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) runs blocking `opencode run` with NO --model flag, so it uses that default. gemma is a small (2B) model that is slow (~30–45s/turn) and may be unreliable at function calling; the recipe uses a crisp tool-forcing prompt and a generous timeout. If gemma simply will not emit a tool call this becomes an infra/model limitation, not an opencode/daemon incapability.

The interactive driver launches a blocking `opencode run` per send and chains --session for continuity; wait_turn is a no-op because the run already blocks. A single tool-using turn (one send + wait_turn + trailing sleep) is within this grammar, so driver_capability: ready.
caveats: OpenCode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs; the driver discovers the ses_… session id by querying SQLite for the most recent row keyed on directory after the first `opencode run` returns.

The local gemma 2B model is slow (~30–45s per turn) and may be unreliable at function calling; the cell records normally if it emits a tool call, otherwise the limitation is the model, not opencode (capabilities.json tool_calls:true).

The phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the auto-executed tool call is asserted via the sustained working span + the no-waiting invariant; the tool part itself is verified in the SQLite store / transcript out-of-band.

sources.note: parseToolPart: tool part status pending/running → function_call (ToolUses), completed/error → function_call_output (ToolResultIDs); parseStepFinish: reason="stop" → turn_done settles working→ready. tool-calls reason stays in working (mid-turn pause).
tool_calls:true; opencode can invoke tools. Headless default `opencode run` auto-executes tools with no interactive gate, so the auto-approved arc never enters waiting.
Source variant ProcessOwnedStore (SQLite at ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db) with ExactName{Name:"opencode"}; daemon polls the session table for the ses_… session.

Sibling committed opencode spec proving the ProcessOwnedStore ready→working→ready arc with session_birth anchored UNPINNED to start (so a transient proc-<PID> presession can't steal identity) and the no-waiting invariant — the exact shape this cell extends with a mid-turn tool call.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated/configured (the default model is set in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json; auth is out-of-band)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

the configured default model must be capable of function calling — if the model emits no tool call this cell is unrecordable for a MODEL reason (opencode capabilities.json tool_calls:true), not an opencode/daemon incapability
setup: The driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ with NO --model flag, so it uses the config default; each send is its own `opencode run` subprocess that exits when the turn completes

No auto-mode / permission step is needed: in headless default `opencode run` auto-executes tools inside opencode's own sandbox with no interactive gate, so the tool call runs autonomously and the session never enters waiting — unlike the claudecode recipe, which must enable auto mode to keep a Bash call from tripping a gate

Session state lives in opencode's SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db, FilesUnderRoot→ProcessOwnedStore), keyed on the UUID ses_… session; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the session table. The tool call lands in the part table as a `tool` part (status pending→running→completed), which the parser maps to function_call / function_call_output (parser.go parseToolPart)

wait_turn is a no-op because `opencode run` already blocks until the turn ends; the ≥4s trailing sleep gives the daemon's poller one full tick to observe the working→ready settle before the run process exits
The send is a crisp single-tool-forcing prompt so the small/slow local default model reliably emits exactly one auto-executed write tool call inside the working span, then replies done

script.text: Create a file named hello.txt containing exactly the text: hello — use your write tool. Then reply with exactly: done

verify: events.jsonl contains the ses_… session born ready within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> presession may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the UUID-keyed ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the ses_… session_id when the tool-using prompt is sent, sustained across the auto-executed write tool call

the opencode SQLite store / transcript.jsonl contains a tool part (function_call / function_call_output) for the write call — the auto-executed, ungated tool call (opencode has no interactive gate in headless default)
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→ready on the same ses_… session_id once the tool resolves and the turn completes (step-finish reason=stop → turn_done)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting for the ses_… session at any point (the call auto-executes; no permission window)
